response_text:
:::writing
At 5:10 each morning, long before the buses arrive and long before the first locker slams open, Luis Ramirez unlocks the side door of Jefferson High School and steps into a building he knows as well as his own home.

He has been the head custodian here for 28 years. In that time the school has had six principals, three new science labs, a rebuilt gym floor, and thousands of students pass through its halls. Ramirez has stayed through all of it, pushing the same wide gray mop bucket down the corridors and greeting students by name as if the building itself had taught him who they were.

“Morning, Tasha. Track meet today, right?”

“Don’t forget your trumpet, Miles. You left it by the band room yesterday.”

Students often pause, confused the first time it happens. Jefferson has nearly 1,400 kids. Teachers sometimes struggle with attendance lists during the first month of school. But Ramirez somehow learns everyone.

Part of it comes from his routine. He walks the entire building every day—cafeteria, science wing, locker rooms, auditorium—checking lights, wiping spills, tightening loose screws on desk legs. In the process he talks to students constantly. Freshmen learn quickly that if you introduce yourself once, he will remember you forever.

“I just pay attention,” he says with a shrug when asked about it. “Kids want to be seen. Names are how you do that.”

Ramirez started at Jefferson in 1997, when his own daughter was in kindergarten across town. The job was supposed to be temporary. He had been working construction and needed steadier hours. But he liked the rhythm of the school day: the quiet before dawn, the surge of noise between classes, the strange calm of a hallway after the final bell.

Over time he became part of the daily landscape. Seniors who graduated years ago still stop by when they’re home from college to shake his hand. Some bring their younger siblings and introduce them: “This is Mr. Ramirez. He knows everybody.”

Teachers rely on him for more than clean floors. When a locker jams, when a classroom smells like a chemistry experiment gone wrong, when a winter storm floods the entrance mats, Ramirez is
